confirmed.title: TaskRACI.userId has no FK relation — orphaned RACI rows, no cascade on user deletion
confirmed.code_evidence:
model TaskRACI {
  id        String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId    String
  userId    String
  role      RACIRole
  createdAt DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task Task @relation(fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, userId, role])
  @@map("task_raci")
}
confirmed.description: TaskRACI stores a userId column and a composite unique constraint on (taskId, userId, role), but there is NO Prisma relation connecting userId to the User table. There is no `user User @relation(...)` in TaskRACI, and the User model (lines 56-97) has no back-relation to TaskRACI. This means: (1) PostgreSQL has no FK constraint on task_raci.user_id — the DB will happily accept a non-existent userId; (2) when a User is deleted, their RACI rows remain in the table as orphaned records with a dangling userId; (3) no onDelete behavior is enforced. Compare with TaskAssignee (lines 360-374) which correctly declares both `task Task @relation(...)` and `user User @relation(...)` with `onDelete: Cascade`.
confirmed.root_cause: The `user` relation side was omitted from TaskRACI when the model was created, leaving userId as a plain String with no FK backing.
confirmed.impact: After a User deletion (soft or hard), stale RACI rows survive referencing a non-existent userId. Queries joining task_raci on users will silently return no validator/responsible data. If userId is reused (UUID collision is astronomically unlikely but app logic may reuse logins), wrong user gets RACI assignment. No cascade means security-sensitive RACI data (RESPONSIBLE, ACCOUNTABLE) is never cleaned up.
confirmed.suggested_fix:
Add the FK relation on TaskRACI and the back-relation on User:

// In model TaskRACI, add:
  user User @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

// In model User, add to the relations block:
  taskRaci TaskRACI[]

Then generate and apply a migration:
  pnpm run db:migrate

The migration will add a FK constraint `REFERENCES users(id)` on task_raci.user_id and a cascade delete trigger consistent with the TaskAssignee pattern.
acceptance_criteria: Prisma schema validates without error after adding the relation
psql: `\d task_raci` shows a FK constraint on user_id REFERENCES users(id)
Deleting a user whose userId appears in task_raci removes those rows (or the FK constraint fires the intended behavior)
User.taskRaci back-relation is accessible from Prisma client queries
confirmed.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM information_schema.referential_constraints WHERE constraint_name LIKE '%task_raci%user%';"
confirmed.notes: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW CONFIRMED. Verified in schema.prisma lines 405-417 verbatim. Init migration (20251116093059_init/migration.sql line 360) adds only task_raci_taskId_fkey, never a userId FK. No subsequent migration adds it. The User model relations block (lines 56-97) has no taskRaci back-relation. Crucially, users.service.ts hard-delete transaction (lines 948-969) lists taskAssignee.deleteMany but NOT taskRACI.deleteMany — RACI rows are silently orphaned on user deletion. Application layer validates user existence on RACI creation (tasks.service.ts line 1029-1035) but provides NO protection against post-creation user deletion.

confirmed.title: TaskDependency.dependsOnTaskId has no index — cascade delete on Task triggers seq-scan
confirmed.code_evidence:
model TaskDependency {
  id              String   @id @default(uuid())
  taskId          String
  dependsOnTaskId String
  createdAt       DateTime @default(now())

  // Relations
  task          Task @relation("TaskDependencies", fields: [taskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  dependsOnTask Task @relation("DependentTasks", fields: [dependsOnTaskId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])
  @@map("task_dependencies")
}
confirmed.description: The composite `@@unique([taskId, dependsOnTaskId])` creates an index with `taskId` as the leading column. Queries and FK checks driven from the `dependsOnTaskId` side (e.g., 'which tasks depend on task X?', or the PostgreSQL FK cascade when deleting a Task referenced as dependsOnTask) cannot use this index and will perform a full seq-scan on task_dependencies. As tasks accumulate, this becomes a lock/latency hotspot on every Task deletion or dependency lookup from the 'dependency target' side.
confirmed.root_cause: Only the forward-direction unique index was declared; no reverse index was added for the `dependsOnTaskId` FK column.
confirmed.impact: Every `DELETE FROM tasks WHERE id = $1` that has rows in task_dependencies (as either taskId or dependsOnTaskId) will seq-scan the table on the dependsOnTaskId side. At scale (thousands of tasks with dependency chains), this produces full-table locks during cascade deletes, potentially blocking the entire task mutation path.
confirmed.suggested_fix:
Add a reverse index:

  @@index([dependsOnTaskId]) // reverse-lookup: tasks that depend on a given task

Migration: pnpm run db:migrate
acceptance_criteria: psql: `\d task_dependencies` shows an index on dependsOnTaskId
EXPLAIN on `SELECT * FROM task_dependencies WHERE "dependsOnTaskId" = $1` uses an index scan
confirmed.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'task_dependencies';"
confirmed.notes: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW CONFIRMED. Verified schema.prisma lines 391-403 verbatim — no @@index([dependsOnTaskId]). Checked ALL migrations referencing task_dependencies (init + DAT-018): init only creates the composite unique index on (taskId, dependsOnTaskId); DAT-018 adds a no-self-loop CHECK and a cycle-prevention trigger — neither adds a dependsOnTaskId index. No migration anywhere adds this index. The DAT-029 pattern (@@index([serviceId]) on UserService, @@index([userId]) on ProjectMember) was not applied here.

confirmed.title: UserSkill.skillId has no standalone index — cascade delete on Skill triggers seq-scan
confirmed.code_evidence:
model UserSkill {
  userId      String
  skillId     String
  level       SkillLevel
  validatedBy String?
  createdAt   DateTime   @default(now())
  updatedAt   DateTime   @updatedAt

  // Relations
  user  User  @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)
  skill Skill @relation(fields: [skillId], references: [id], onDelete: Cascade)

  @@id([userId, skillId])
  @@map("user_skills")
}
confirmed.description: The composite PK `@@id([userId, skillId])` creates a B-tree index with `userId` as the leading column. Looking up 'all users with skill X' (the reverse direction) or firing the `onDelete: Cascade` FK enforcement when deleting a Skill row requires scanning the entire `user_skills` table on the skillId column. The DAT-029 annotation explicitly added @@index([serviceId]) to UserService for the same structural reason; UserSkill was not covered.
confirmed.root_cause: The composite PK only covers the userId-leading direction; no standalone index was declared for skillId.
confirmed.impact: Deleting a Skill (e.g., when decommissioning an obsolete skill) causes PostgreSQL to seq-scan user_skills to cascade-delete dependent UserSkill rows. For organizations with many users and skills, this can cause table-level lock escalation. Querying 'which users have skill X' (a workload-planning query) also seq-scans.
confirmed.suggested_fix:
Add a reverse index:

  @@index([skillId]) // DAT-029 pattern: reverse-lookup 'all users with skill X'

Migration: pnpm run db:migrate
acceptance_criteria: psql: `\d user_skills` shows an index on skillId
EXPLAIN on `SELECT * FROM user_skills WHERE "skillId" = $1` uses an index scan
confirmed.verification_command: psql $DATABASE_URL -c "SELECT indexname FROM pg_indexes WHERE tablename = 'user_skills';"
confirmed.notes: ADVERSARIAL REVIEW CONFIRMED. Verified schema.prisma lines 763-777 verbatim — no @@index([skillId]). Checked all migrations: no migration adds a skillId index on user_skills. The @@id([userId, skillId]) PK only covers userId-leading lookups. Finding is structurally identical to DAT-029 pattern that was applied to UserService but missed UserSkill.

refuted.reason: The decision to keep ProjectMember.role as a free String was explicitly documented and implemented as DAT-035 (migration 20260528160000_dat035_project_member_role_length). The DAT-012 audit intentionally bailed on enum promotion for this column because the value space is genuinely open by design — per-collectivité role titles vary ('Responsable infra', 'Référente support', 'Lead dev' alongside 'Chef de projet', 'Membre'). A DB CHECK constraint (project_members_role_length_ck: char_length between 1 and 100) provides a structural floor. The DTO AddMemberDto (apps/api/src/projects/dto/add-member.dto.ts) has a companion @Length(1,100) + @Transform(trim) validator. The finding's premise that this should be an enum directly contradicts the documented project audit decision. The medium confidence was warranted — the '...' comment IS intentional extensibility.

The XOR CHECK constraint already exists. Migration 20260411100717_add_third_parties_and_time_entry_actor_xor (lines 168-174) explicitly adds 'time_entries_actor_xor_check' CHECK ((userId IS NOT NULL AND thirdPartyId IS NULL) OR (userId IS NULL AND thirdPartyId IS NOT NULL)). This is the exact constraint the finding suggests adding. The constraint was added as part of the third-party actor extension, before the audit. The finding's schema.prisma evidence is correct (PSL cannot express CHECK constraints) but the suggested raw SQL migration was already applied.
